variables.content: Numerical derivatives of finite-N DMC energy bands are unstable at k≈k_F: the derivative (dE/dk) evaluated from discrete finite-N DMC data often exhibits outlier points in the immediate vicinity of the Fermi surface, a pathology attributable to the combination of (i) the divergence or pathological behavior of the Hartree-Fock band derivative at k_F and (ii) DMC in a finite cell retrieving only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy when using a fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave function; consequently, robust estimates of the quasiparticle effective mass are obtained by fitting the band over a finite wave-number range Δk about k_F (using, for example, the quartic parametrization E(k)=α_0+α_2 k^2+α_4 k^4) and using the fit-derived derivative at k_F to average over and suppress the near-k_F anomalies.

In diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations of finite periodic simulation cells using fixed-node Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions, the DMC total-energy estimator recovers only a finite fraction of the exact correlation energy in a finite cell; combined with the fact that the derivative of the Hartree-Fock single-particle band with respect to k is divergent or pathological at the Fermi wave vector k_F, this incomplete recovery of correlation energy in a finite cell leads to locally anomalous or outlier numerical estimates of the band derivative (dE/dk) at k≈k_F when derivatives are computed from discrete finite-N DMC data, producing the observed unstable near-k_F derivative points.

For DMC-computed discrete single-quasiparticle bands \\(\\mathcal{E}(k)\\) obtained in finite periodic simulation cells for a 2D HEG, define the pathological behaviour as the erratic variation of estimated quasiparticle effective mass \\(m^{*}\\) when the derivative \\(d\\mathcal{E}/dk\\) is estimated by fitting only a very narrow \\(k\\)-range immediately surrounding the Fermi wavevector \\(k_{F}\\). The proposition is: this near-\\(k_{F}\\) pathological behaviour of \\(m^{*}\\) estimates arises primarily from finite-cell fluctuations (for example, the discrete sampling of \\(\\mathbf{k}\\)-points and cell-induced finite-size correlation effects) together with Hartree–Fock-like non-smooth mean-field features that persist in finite cells, rather than from an intrinsic non-differentiability or singularity of the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle band at \\(k_{F}\\); this explains why expanding the \\(k\\)-range used in the fit reduces the pathology.

For discrete DMC single-particle band data E(k_i) in a periodic finite simulation cell, performing a quartic fit over a moderately wide wave-number interval k∈[k_F−Δk,k_F+Δk]—meaning Δk large enough to include multiple quantized k points on both sides of k_F rather than being restricted to an infinitesimal neighborhood of k_F—reduces sensitivity to localized anomalous fluctuations or outlier derivative estimates in the immediate vicinity of k_F and produces per-size effective-mass estimates that are more stable with respect to statistical noise and more amenable to controlled finite-size extrapolation.

Fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) total energies E^{\mathrm{DMC}} of finite spherical jellium clusters depend primarily on the nodal surface of the trial many-body wave function and are variationally insensitive to small changes in the underlying single-particle electron density profile n(\mathbf{r}); therefore, to obtain an effective DMC exchange-correlation energy evaluated on a specified density profile, one may subtract from E^{\mathrm{DMC}} the Hartree and kinetic (non-exchange-correlation) contributions computed for the LSD density n_{\mathrm{LSD}}(\mathbf{r}) to define an effective DMC exchange-correlation energy on n_{\mathrm{LSD}}, provided the density differences between the implicit DMC density and n_{\mathrm{LSD}} are small compared to the energy scales of interest.

In finite periodic simulation cells for a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas using Slater-Jastrow-backflow trial wave functions that contain variational parameters in the Jastrow and backflow factors, reoptimizing those variational parameters for an excited-state configuration (created by adding or removing an electron in a specified Bloch state) can yield a lower total DMC energy in that finite cell relative to using the ground-state-optimized parameters; because, in the thermodynamic limit, the optimal short-range correlation functions are unchanged by a single excitation, this finite-cell energy lowering is a manifestation of finite-size error and can reduce computed excitation bandwidths and therefore amplify finite-size bias in bandwidth-derived quantities such as the quasiparticle effective mass.

Let E^{\mathrm{LDA}}_{\mathrm{conv}} denote the LDA total energy computed with dense k-point sampling (converged with respect to k points) and E^{\mathrm{LDA}}_{\mathrm{cell}} denote the LDA total energy computed with the k-point sampling corresponding to a given finite periodic DMC simulation cell; define the correction \Delta_{\mathbf{k}}=E^{\mathrm{LDA}}_{\mathrm{conv}}-E^{\mathrm{LDA}}_{\mathrm{cell}}. The proposition is that adding \Delta_{\mathbf{k}} to raw many-body DMC total energies computed in the finite periodic simulation cell removes most of the single-particle finite-size error and yields corrected DMC energies that are closer to the infinite-size many-body result, so that the k-point correction derived from one-particle LDA calculations is an effective proxy for reducing single-particle finite-size bias in the DMC slab data.

Measured against the DMC benchmark correlation energies for the systems studied, coupled-cluster with single, double, and perturbative triple excitations (CCSD(T)) recovers approximately 75% of the DMC-observed valence correlation energy for the $C_{10}$ isomers evaluated with the limited bases used, variational Monte Carlo (VMC) using the optimized Slater-Jastrow trial functions recovers about 85% of the DMC valence correlation energy for the studied carbon systems, and separate empirical estimates from smaller carbon systems indicate that DMC recovers on the order of 95% of the valence correlation energy; thus, under the reported bases and trial-function quality, DMC provides a substantially more complete treatment of valence correlation than CCSD(T) in limited bases and than mean-field methods.

For a periodic diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculation of the total electronic energy performed on a simulation supercell containing N atoms, let E_N denote the raw DMC total energy of that N-atom supercell (evaluated per supercell), let E_N^{DFT} denote the Kohn–Sham density functional theory (DFT) total energy computed for the identical supercell geometry and k-point sampling used in the DMC calculation, and let E_\infty^{DFT} denote the DFT total energy per primitive cell computed with fully converged Brillouin-zone (k-point) sampling; the DFT-based finite-size corrected DMC energy is defined as E_N^{c} = E_N + (E_\infty^{DFT} − E_N^{DFT}), and the proposition is that this correction removes the dominant one-body finite-size error present in E_N so that the residual finite-size error in E_N^{c} is small (on the order of a few meV per formula unit for the materials and supercell sizes used) and therefore amenable to a simple extrapolation to N → ∞.

For paramagnetic 3D-HEG calculations performed in a finite periodic simulation cell containing N = 178 electrons, the Slater-Jastrow (SJ) trial wave function—defined as a single Slater determinant of plane-wave orbitals multiplied by a Jastrow correlation factor—and the Slater-Jastrow-backflow (SJB) trial wave function—the SJ form with additional backflow-transformed quasiparticle coordinates—yield very similar diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) quasiparticle effective masses m*. Specifically, at density parameter r_s = 1 (r_s = ((3/(4π n))^{1/3} a_B^{-1})), the DMC values are m* = 0.915(1) with the SJ trial WF and m* = 0.921(1) with the SJB trial WF, and at r_s = 10 the DMC values are m* = 0.75(1) (SJ) and m* = 0.78(1) (SJB); these numerical results indicate that, for the paramagnetic 3D-HEG at N = 178 and the densities studied, inclusion of backflow in the trial WF produces only small quantitative changes in the DMC effective mass.
